summary: DeVault presents findings from months of scraping the Hacker News API and website to analyze how moderators and users influence content visibility. He documents the tools available to HN moderators—title editing, rank manipulation, post killing/deletion, and shadowbanning—with direct quotes from moderator Dan clarifying their usage. He finds that while moderator actions are generally benign, user flagging is clearly abused as a de facto downvote mechanism, removing legitimate posts with significant engagement. He argues that HN's moderation opacity is a structural problem and proposes a public audit log, moderator review of flagged posts, and community-appointed moderators to improve transparency.
key_points: HN moderators have a tiered toolkit ranging from subtle downweighting to full deletion, with downweighting being the most frequent action
User flagging is actively abused to suppress legitimate content, functioning as an unsanctioned downvote on stories users disagree with
Moderator actions are opaque by design—reasoning is rarely public, and private email is the only reliable channel for appeals
The submitter is not the only stakeholder when a post is moderated; upvoters and commenters also lose when content silently disappears
A public audit log of moderator actions would improve accountability without requiring moderators to manually explain every decision
money_quotes: The main issue with flags is that they're often used as an alternative to the HN's (by design) lack of a downvoting feature.
Moderation on HN is a black box - most of the time, moderators don't make the reasoning behind their actions clear.
I don't think that private discourse between the submitter and the moderators is the only solution.
The submitter is not the only one whose interests are at stake.
Other people may be invested in the topic, too - users who upvoted the story might not notice its disappearance, but would like more attention drawn to the topic and enjoy more discussion.
I should again emphasize that most moderator actions are benign and agreeable. They do a great job on the whole, but striving to do even better would be admirable.
Hacker News is a great place for just that - hacker news. It has been for a long time and I hope it continues to be. Let's work together on running it transparently to the benefit of all.
key_insight: HN's moderation is mostly competent but structurally opaque, and user flagging serves as an unaccountable censorship mechanism that removes legitimate content without review.
